content.text: Connecting LLMs to Databases

Databases are the most common external data source in enterprise LLM applications. An LLM on its own cannot query a database — it has no connection, no schema awareness, and no knowledge of your data. Wrapping database operations as tools gives the LLM a controlled, auditable interface to your data: it calls a tool with typed arguments, and the tool executes the query and returns results as text.
There are two main patterns:
Pre-defined query tools
— each tool wraps a specific, parameterised query (get_order_by_id, list_customers_by_region). Safer, more predictable, easier to audit.
Text-to-SQL
— the LLM generates a SQL query string from natural language, which is then executed. More flexible, but requires schema injection and careful validation.
Start with pre-defined query tools. They eliminate SQL injection risk, are trivial to test, and cover 80% of use cases. Add text-to-SQL only when the query space is genuinely open-ended.
Pre-defined Query Tools

The safest database integration pattern: define one tool per query, use parameterised arguments, and never let the LLM touch raw SQL. The LLM chooses which tool to call and what arguments to pass — the SQL lives entirely in your code.
Text-to-SQL

Text-to-SQL lets the LLM write SQL from a natural language question. To do this reliably, inject the database schema into the prompt. The schema tells the LLM the table names, column names, types, and relationships it needs to construct a syntactically valid query.
Schema Injection

from sqlalchemy import create_engine, inspect

engine = create_engine("sqlite:///sales.db")

def get_schema_prompt(engine) -> str:
    """Generate a schema description for the LLM from SQLAlchemy metadata."""
    inspector = inspect(engine)
    lines = ["Database schema:"]
    for table_name in inspector.get_table_names():
        cols = inspector.get_columns(table_name)
        col_defs = ", ".join(f"{c['name']} {c['type']}" for c in cols)
        lines.append(f"  {table_name}({col_defs})")
    return "\n".join(lines)

# Example output:
# Database schema:
#   orders(id TEXT, customer_name TEXT, total FLOAT, status TEXT, created_at DATETIME)
#   products(id TEXT, name TEXT, price FLOAT, category TEXT, stock INTEGER)
#   customers(id TEXT, name TEXT, email TEXT, region TEXT)

schema_prompt = get_schema_prompt(engine)
print(schema_prompt)
Text-to-SQL Tool
NoSQL and Key-Value Databases

Not all databases are relational. LLMs can query document stores, key-value caches, and time-series databases through the same tool pattern. The key difference: instead of SQL, the tool arguments map to the database's native query interface.
Redis
MongoDB
Security and Safety
Database tools carry real risks. A bug in your tool or a misbehaving LLM could corrupt or leak data. Apply these controls before shipping:
Risk
Control
SQL injection via text-to-SQL
Regex-block write keywords; run query in read-only DB user
Excessive data exposure
Select only necessary columns; cap row count (50-100 rows max)
PII in tool results
Mask or omit sensitive columns (SSN, card number, password hash) in the SELECT list
Runaway queries
Set query timeout (SQLAlchemy: connect_args={'timeout': 5})
Write operations via LLM
Use a read-only DB user for all LLM-accessible connections
Schema leakage
Only inject schema for tables the LLM needs; keep internal tables out
LangChain SQLDatabase Toolkit

LangChain ships a ready-made toolkit for SQL databases that handles schema injection and provides a suite of tools: one to list tables, one to describe a table's schema, and one to execute a query. Use it when you want text-to-SQL without writing the plumbing yourself.

# pip install langchain-community langchain-openai
from langchain_community.utilities import SQLDatabase
from langchain_community.agent_toolkits import SQLDatabaseToolkit
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent

db = SQLDatabase.from_uri("sqlite:///sales.db")
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

toolkit = SQLDatabaseToolkit(db=db, llm=llm)
tools = toolkit.get_tools()
# tools = [sql_db_list_tables, sql_db_schema, sql_db_query, sql_db_query_checker]

system_prompt = (
    "You are a data analyst. Use the SQL tools to answer questions about the sales database. "
    "Always check the table schema before querying. Never run destructive statements."
)

agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools, prompt=system_prompt)
result = agent.invoke({
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Which product category had the highest revenue last month?"}]
})
print(result["messages"][-1].content)
Database tools give LLMs live access to your data.

Pre-defined query tools are the safest starting point — no SQL generation, no injection risk, fully auditable. Add text-to-SQL with schema injection when the query space is open-ended, and always run on a read-only connection. Mask PII columns at the query layer, not in post-processing.
